question:
Problem B-3
Prove or disprove the following statement: If \( F \) is a finite set with two or more elements, then there exists a binary operation * on \( F \) such that for all \( x, y, z \) in \( F \).
(i) \( x * z=y * z \) implies \( x=y \) (right cancellation holds). and
(ii) \( x *(y * z) \neq(x * y) * z \) (no case of associativity holds).
solution:
B-3.
The statement is true. Let \( \phi \) be any bijection on \( F \) with no fixed points, and set \( x * y=\phi(x) \).

descriptive_long.question:
Problem B-3
Prove or disprove the following statement: If \( setfinite \) is a finite set with two or more elements, then there exists a binary operation * on \( setfinite \) such that for all \( elementalpha, elementbeta, elementgamma \) in \( setfinite \).
(i) \( elementalpha * elementgamma=elementbeta * elementgamma \) implies \( elementalpha=elementbeta \) (right cancellation holds). and
(ii) \( elementalpha *(elementbeta * elementgamma) \neq(elementalpha * elementbeta) * elementgamma \) (no case of associativity holds).
descriptive_long.solution:
B-3.
The statement is true. Let \( bijection \) be any bijection on \( setfinite \) with no fixed points, and set \( elementalpha * elementbeta=bijection(elementalpha) \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Problem B-3
Prove or disprove the following statement: If \( snowglade \) is a finite set with two or more elements, then there exists a binary operation * on \( snowglade \) such that for all \( pinestake, driftbloom, cragbridge \) in \( snowglade \).
(i) \( pinestake * cragbridge=driftbloom * cragbridge \) implies \( pinestake=driftbloom \) (right cancellation holds). and
(ii) \( pinestake *(driftbloom * cragbridge) \neq(pinestake * driftbloom) * cragbridge \) (no case of associativity holds).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
B-3.
The statement is true. Let \( windharbor \) be any bijection on \( snowglade \) with no fixed points, and set \( pinestake * driftbloom=windharbor(pinestake) \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Problem B-3
Prove or disprove the following statement: If \( infiniteset \) is a finite set with two or more elements, then there exists a binary operation * on \( infiniteset \) such that for all \( fixedvalue, constantvalue, stationaryval \) in \( infiniteset \).
(i) \( fixedvalue * stationaryval = constantvalue * stationaryval \) implies \( fixedvalue = constantvalue \) (right cancellation holds). and
(ii) \( fixedvalue *(constantvalue * stationaryval) \neq(fixedvalue * constantvalue) * stationaryval \) (no case of associativity holds).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
B-3.
The statement is true. Let \( identitymap \) be any bijection on \( infiniteset \) with no fixed points, and set \( fixedvalue * constantvalue = identitymap(fixedvalue) \).

garbled_string.question:
Problem B-3
Prove or disprove the following statement: If \( qzxwvtnp \) is a finite set with two or more elements, then there exists a binary operation * on \( qzxwvtnp \) such that for all \( mnlopqrs, tuvwxabc, ghijklst \) in \( qzxwvtnp \).
(i) \( mnlopqrs * ghijklst=tuvwxabc * ghijklst \) implies \( mnlopqrs=tuvwxabc \) (right cancellation holds). and
(ii) \( mnlopqrs *(tuvwxabc * ghijklst) \neq(mnlopqrs * tuvwxabc) * ghijklst \) (no case of associativity holds).
garbled_string.solution:
B-3.
The statement is true. Let \( hjgrksla \) be any bijection on \( qzxwvtnp \) with no fixed points, and set \( mnlopqrs * tuvwxabc=hjgrksla(mnlopqrs) \).

kernel_variant.question:
Let $F=\mathbb{N}= \{0,1,2,\ldots\}$.  
For $n\ge 1$ an \emph{$n$-fold $\ast$-expression} is a formal word
\[
x_{1}\ast x_{2}\ast\cdots\ast x_{n},
\]
together with a choice of binary parenthesisation (there are
\[
C_{\,n-1}\;=\;\frac{1}{n}\binom{2n-2}{\,n-1\,}
\]
such choices, the $(n-1)$-st Catalan number).

Construct an explicit binary operation
\[
\ast \; :\; F\times F \;\longrightarrow\; F
\]
having all three properties below.

1.\; (two-sided cancellation)  
   $\;x\ast y = x\ast z \;\Longrightarrow\; y=z$
   and  
   $y\ast x = z\ast x \;\Longrightarrow\; y=z$
   for every $x,y,z\in F$;

2.\; (total non-associativity)  
   For every integer $n\ge 3$ and every $n$-tuple
   $(x_{1},\dots ,x_{n})$ of elements of $F$, the
   $C_{\,n-1}$ different parenthesised values of the word
   $x_{1}\ast\cdots\ast x_{n}$ are \emph{pairwise distinct};
   equivalently, no two distinct binary terms of length $\ge 3$
   coincide under any simultaneous substitution of the variables
   by elements of $F$;

3.\; $F$ contains  

   (i)  no one-sided identity for $\ast$,  

   (ii) no two-sided identity for $\ast$,  

   (iii) no idempotent element for $\ast$.

Give a complete proof that your construction satisfies all three requirements.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout, let $F=\mathbb{N}$.  Define the encoding
\[
\pi : \mathbb{N}\times\mathbb{N}\;\longrightarrow\;\mathbb{N},
\qquad
\pi(a,b)=2^{a}\,3^{b}.
\tag{1}
\]
Set
\[
a\ast b \;:=\; \pi(a,b)=2^{a}\,3^{b},
\qquad a,b\in\mathbb{N}.
\tag{2}
\]

\medskip
\textbf{Step 1.  Two-sided cancellation.}  
Unique prime factorisation implies that $\pi$ is injective in each coordinate:
if $2^{x}\,3^{y}=2^{x}\,3^{z}$ then $y=z$, and if
$2^{x}\,3^{y}=2^{z}\,3^{y}$ then $x=z$.
Hence
\[
a\ast b=a\ast c \Longrightarrow 2^{a}\,3^{b}=2^{a}\,3^{c}\Longrightarrow b=c,
\]
and analogously $b\ast a=c\ast a\Longrightarrow b=c$.  Thus both left and
right cancellation hold.

\medskip
\textbf{Step 2.  Total non-associativity.}  
Represent each binary parenthesisation of
$x_{1}\ast\cdots\ast x_{n}$ by a rooted, ordered binary tree whose
leaves are, from left to right, $x_{1},\ldots ,x_{n}$.
Denote the set of such trees by $\mathcal{T}$.
Define recursively a valuation
\[
\varphi:\mathcal{T}\longrightarrow\mathbb{N}
\]
by
\[
\varphi(\text{leaf labelled }x_{i})=x_{i},\qquad
\varphi(T_{1}\circ T_{2})=
\varphi(T_{1})\ast\varphi(T_{2})=
2^{\varphi(T_{1})}\,3^{\varphi(T_{2})},
\]
where $T_{1}\circ T_{2}$ is a tree whose root has left
sub-tree $T_{1}$ and right sub-tree $T_{2}$.

\smallskip
\emph{Claim:} $\varphi$ is injective.

\smallskip
\emph{Proof by induction on the number of internal nodes.}

Base case (one leaf): trivial.

Induction step: let $T,S\in\mathcal{T}$ have at least one internal node and
assume $\varphi(T)=\varphi(S)$.  Write
$T=T_{1}\circ T_{2}$ and $S=S_{1}\circ S_{2}$.
Then
\[
2^{\varphi(T_{1})}\,3^{\varphi(T_{2})}
=
2^{\varphi(S_{1})}\,3^{\varphi(S_{2})}.
\]
Uniqueness of prime factorisation yields
$\varphi(T_{1})=\varphi(S_{1})$ and
$\varphi(T_{2})=\varphi(S_{2})$.
By the induction hypothesis $T_{1}=S_{1}$ and $T_{2}=S_{2}$, whence $T=S$.
\hfill $\square$

\smallskip
Thus different trees, i.e.\ different parenthesisations,
always give different numerical values, and
non-associativity is complete: already for $n=3$
\[
x\ast(y\ast z)=2^{x}\,3^{2^{y}3^{z}}
\neq
2^{2^{x}3^{y}}\,3^{z}=(x\ast y)\ast z,
\]
and no collisions occur for longer expressions.

\medskip
\textbf{Step 3.  Absence of identities and idempotents.}

\smallskip
(i) \emph{No right identity.}  
Suppose $e$ satisfied $x\ast e=x$ for every $x\in F$.
Then $2^{x}\,3^{e}=x$ for all $x$.
Taking $x=0$ gives $3^{e}=0$, impossible in $\mathbb{N}$.

\smallskip
(ii) \emph{No left identity.}  
If $e\ast x=x$ for every $x$, then $2^{e}\,3^{x}=x$ for all $x$.
Putting $x=0$ yields $2^{e}=0$, again impossible.

\smallskip
(iii) \emph{No idempotent.}  
An idempotent $a$ would satisfy $a\ast a=a$, i.e.
\[
2^{a}\,3^{a}=a \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 6^{a}=a.
\]
But $6^{a}\ge 6>a$ for every $a\ge 1$, while $a=0$ gives $1\neq 0$.
Hence no solution exists.

\medskip
\textbf{Conclusion.}  
The operation \eqref{2} satisfies two-sided cancellation,
is totally non-associative, and admits neither one- nor two-sided identities
nor idempotents.  All three requirements of the corrected problem are met.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
Compared with the original problem and its kernel variant, the present task
demands several major additional layers of sophistication.

• Two–sided cancellation replaces one–sided cancellation, forcing the
  constructor to invent an operation that can be inverted in both arguments.

• “Total non-associativity’’ is vastly stronger than merely exhibiting a
  single triple violating associativity; one must guarantee that no binary
  identity of any length (indeed, none of the Cₙ₋₁ parenthesisations) can
  coincide.  This needs an injective encoding of full binary-tree structure,
  far subtler than the brief argument of the original.

• Extra algebraic restrictions (no identities, no idempotents) exclude a host
  of cheap constructions that might accidentally introduce units or fixed
  points; the constructor must therefore control additional arithmetic
  properties of the operation.

• The proof now relies on unique prime factorisation, recursion on binary
  trees and Catalan-number counting—techniques absent from the original
  problem—raising both the conceptual and computational load needed for a
  complete solution.

Together these enhancements make the new variant significantly more
challenging and mathematically richer than either previous version.
